segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, establishing a medieval or religious ambiance, accompanied by a somber, instrumental background score. A Man in Red Tunic confronts a Young Man in Beige Tunic holding a mug, while a Woman in White Tunic enters wielding a long wooden staff. The background music continues as the Man in Red Tunic and the Woman in White Tunic engage in a brief mock sword fight, with sounds of movement and clashing, while the Young Man in Beige Tunic observes. Following the combat, the Man in Red Tunic and the Young Man in Beige Tunic exit stage left, and the Woman in White Tunic exits stage right. A new character, a Woman in a long black robe over a white tunic, then enters from the center arch, holding a book. She walks to the center of the stage, and in a clear, theatrical female voice, begins reading aloud, stating, "I pray you, all give your audience, and hear this matter with reverence. By figure, a moral play. The summoning of every man, called it is. That of our lives and ending shows, how transitory we be all day." She continues, "This matter is wonders precious, but the intent of it is more gracious, and swift to bear away. The story saith, man, in the beginning, look well and take good heed to thy ending, be ye never so gay." She then closes the book, places it on a small table, removes her robe to reveal the white tunic underneath, and continues to speak expressively to the attentive audience, moving across the stage, as the lighting remains focused on her.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Woman in White Tunic, dressed in a dark tunic with a red sash and light-colored pants, barefoot, standing center stage on a raised platform. She faces the audience, speaking expressively with hand gestures, her theatrical and commanding voice declaring, "Oh my God, I am here at your will, your commandment to fulfill. Go down to every man, and show him in my name, a pilgrimage he must on him take, which he in no wise may escape. And that he bring with him a sure reckoning, without delay or any tarrying." The Stage Backdrop features three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, while Audience Members watch attentively in the foreground. After her speech, the Woman in White Tunic turns and walks off stage to the left. A new figure, the Woman in Black Robe, enters from the right, wearing a long black robe over a white tunic, barefoot, and holding a scythe upright. She stands center stage, her solemn, dramatic voice proclaiming, "Lord, I will in the world go run overall, and truly, I shall out search both the great and the small. He that liveth beastly out of God's laws and dreadeth not folly. Every man that loveth riches, I shall strike with my dart, his sight for to blind, from heaven to depart. All except that alms be his good friend, then in hell for to dwell, world without end." She occasionally gestures with the scythe. After a moment, she turns and walks off stage to the left. Immediately following, the Young Man in Beige Tunic enters from the right, wearing a beige tunic and brown pants, his footsteps audible as he walks across the stage toward the center before exiting to the left. The Woman in Black Robe's voice then continues from off-stage, stating, "And lo, yonder I see every man walking..." The lighting remains consistent throughout, focused on the stage, maintaining a theatrical and dramatic atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A woman in a long black robe, barefoot, holding a scythe, moves from the left toward the center, her solemn and authoritative presence emphasized as she begins to speak in a dramatic, formal female voice, declaring, "Think upon my coming. His mind upon his fleshly lusts and treasure. Great pain they shall cause him to endure before the Lord Heaven's King." She then challenges a young man in a beige tunic, black belt, and brown pants, who stands on the right, initially observing her with hands on hips, asking, "Hast thou thy maker forgot? Why askest thou? What is it thou wit?" As she turns to face him, he gestures expressively while she reveals her divine mission, stating, "I am sent to thee hastily from God above his majesty." The man, surprised, interjects with a male voice, "What? Sent to me?" The woman confirms, "Certainly," and ominously adds, "here we depart, ye shall know," before explaining that "A reckoning he will needs have without any legal respite." The man, now stepping forward and gesturing, pleads for more time, "To give a reckoning longer leisure I crave. This blind matter troubleth my wit." The woman, holding her scythe upright, delivers her verdict: "On thee, thou must take a long journey," before the audio cuts off. Throughout this intense dialogue, the audience watches attentively in the foreground, and the consistent lighting highlights the dramatic interaction.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Two men engage in a dramatic, theatrical dialogue. The Man in Beige Tunic, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and holding a wooden staff, speaks with a formal, cautious tone, questioning a 'voyage' and stating, "I know it well, it should be to my pain," and later, "If faith I will not that way go." He gestures expressively, adjusts his hat, and clasps his hands. The Young Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and holding a dark object from which he drinks, responds with an assertive, challenging tone, reminding the other, "Ye said if I had need, ye would never forsake me," and dramatically declares, "Indeed, Death was with me here." He points and moves animatedly across the stage. Their intense conversation continues, with the Man in Beige Tunic further debating, "If Death be the messenger, for no man living today would I set foot on that loath journey," and the Young Man asserting, "Your mind will sooner apply to go to mirth and solace and play, than to bear me company in my long journey." The audience watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the performers under consistent, focused lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A Man in Beige Tunic, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and holding a wooden staff, stands center stage, speaking expressively with a theatrical male voice, urging listeners to 'eat and drink and make good cheer,' and to 'trust me, verily.' A Young Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and dressed similarly, enters from the right, holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup from which he drinks. He responds to the Man in Beige Tunic with a distinct, slightly more refined male voice, remarking, 'Oh, that is simply enticing indeed.' The Man in Beige Tunic then pleads, 'Now, gentle fellow, help me in my necessity... Now, gentle fellowship, remember me,' his voice growing urgent as he declares, 'by Saint John I will not with thee go!' The Young Man, now explicitly addressed as 'Fellowship,' replies, 'Yet I pray thee, take the labor and do so much for me,' his tone somewhat dismissive. The Man in Beige Tunic desperately calls out, 'Fellowship, grant mercy.' Fellowship, becoming agitated, rejects him, stating, 'Nay, if thou hadst not tarried, I would not have forsaken thee so. So now, as God speed thee on thy journey, I will depart from thee, as fast as I may.' The Man in Beige Tunic pleads again, 'Whither away, Fellowship? Will ye forsake me?' to which Fellowship confirms, 'Yea, by my faith, to God I betake thee.' Fellowship then delivers a somber farewell, 'Farewell, good Fellowship, for this my heart is sore,' as he continues to interact dynamically, moving between the arches and occasionally addressing the attentive audience. The lighting remains focused on the performers, highlighting their dramatic interaction against the dark backdrop, suggesting a theatrical performance with a spiritual or historical theme.

Her clear female voice interjects, asking, "What account is it that ye must render?" The Man in Red Tunic responds, stating, "Of all my works, I must show how I have lived." segments.integrated_caption: On a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, three performers engage in a dramatic scene. The Woman in White Tunic, in a white tunic with a red vest, brown pants, and holding a long staff, turns to the Woman in Brown Cloak (white tunic, brown cloak, barefoot, staff) and speaks in a clear female voice, stating, "She loves to go to feast, there to be nice, and to dance abroad to start. I will give her leave to join you in that journey, if that you and she may agree." The Man in Beige Tunic (wide-brimmed hat, beige tunic, wooden staff) observes, then responds in a male voice, asking, "No, show me the very effect of your mind. Will you not go with me or abide behind?" The Woman in Brown Cloak replies in a distinct female voice, "Abide behind. Yea, that will I, and I may. Therefore, farewell, till another day." Following this exchange, the Woman in White Tunic exits stage right. The Man in Beige Tunic and Woman in Brown Cloak continue their interaction. The Man in Beige Tunic then speaks expressively in a male voice, lamenting, "Alas, shall I never be merry or glad? For fair promises meant to be make, yet when I have need, they me forsake. I am deceived, that maketh me sad." During his speech, he gestures and adjusts his hat. A distinct "clunk" sound is heard as he places his staff on the ground, followed by a brief, dramatic laugh. He then takes the Woman in Brown Cloak’s staff, helps her put on his hat, and she dons his cloak. The Woman in Brown Cloak, now wearing the Man's hat and cloak, delivers a farewell in her distinct female voice, saying, "Cousin every man, farewell now, for verily I will not go with you... Now God keep thee for now I go." After her speech, she exits stage right, leaving the Man in Beige Tunic alone center stage, holding his staff. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the action. The lighting remains consistent, focused on the performers, enhancing the dramatic tone of the scene.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, holding a wooden staff, standing center stage on a raised platform. He speaks expressively to the attentive audience, gesturing with his hands and occasionally adjusting his hat, against a backdrop of three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, while melancholic, medieval-style instrumental music plays softly. He delivers a theatrical monologue with a mournful and dramatic tone, lamenting, "Ah, Jesu, is all come here to love fair words make fools me. They promise and nothing will do certain." He continues, "My kinsmen promised me faithfully to abide by me steadfastly. Yet fast away do they flee. Even so, fellowship promised me," his voice conveying a sense of betrayal. He then shifts, stating, "Yet, in my mind a thing there is. All my life I have loved riches. If that my good would help me might, it would make my heart full light. I will speak to him in this distress," before dramatically asking, "Where art thou, my goods and riches?" Around the 50-second mark, the Man in Red Tunic, wearing a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, enters from the right, holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup. He walks toward the center stage, gesturing expressively as he approaches the Man in Beige Tunic, and with a deeper, more resonant voice, questions, "Who calleth me? Everyman? What haste hast thou?" initiating an interaction between the two performers as the music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, further enhanced by a small cross on the left arch and a lantern on the right. A Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, a wide-brimmed hat, and a brown cloak, stands barefoot center stage, holding a wooden staff. He speaks expressively with a clear, theatrical, and dramatic voice, delivering a monologue about his piled-up possessions and inability to serve, while gesturing with his hands and occasionally adjusting his hat or pointing upward. A consistent, melancholic, and dramatic background music with string instruments plays softly. To his left, a Man in Red Tunic, wearing a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, enters from the right, approaching the Man in Beige Tunic while holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup. The Man in Red Tunic, with a distinct, theatrical, and slightly urgent voice, interjects, asking, "What will he have?" The Man in Beige Tunic then invites him closer for "counsel." The two engage in animated conversation, with the Man in Red Tunic gesturing emphatically and moving between the arches, offering to remedy "sorrow or adversity" but then clarifying that "It is another disease that grieveth me." The Man in Beige Tunic responds, stating, "It is not of this world, I tell thee so," and continues to speak of being "sent for another way to go" to give an account before "the highest Jupiter of all." He then expresses, "And all my life, I have loved thee," before imploring the Man in Red Tunic to "go with me" to "help clean and purify" his "reckoning," concluding with a proverb about money. Throughout, the audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the action, as the stage lighting highlights the performers against the dark backdrop.

Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a wide-brimmed hat, beige tunic, light pants, and a brown cloak, stands holding a wooden staff. He speaks expressively in a theatrical, archaic tone, lamenting, "Oh goods. Thou hast had long my hearty love, which should have been given to the Lord above." He then asks, "But wilt thou not go with me? I pray thee, truth to say." To his right, the Man in Red Tunic, in a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, observes and occasionally gestures, responding in a deeper, equally theatrical voice, "No, so God me speed. Therefore, farewell, and have a good day." The Man in Beige Tunic then walks forward, steps down from the platform, and sits on a black box in front of the stage, placing his hat on his lap. As melancholic, lute-like instrumental music begins, he continues his monologue, using hand gestures and occasionally pointing, holding his staff. He laments, "Oh, to whom might I haste my moan to make? For to find company in that long journey," recalling how "First, fellowship. He said he would with me go. His words were very pleasant and gay, but afterward, he left me alone." He further recounts, "Then spake I to my kinsmen, all in despair. They also gave me words fair. They lacked no fair speaking, but all forsake me in the ending." His narrative continues, expressing a search for comfort from his "goods," which also proved fruitless. The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the engaging, theatrical performance, highlighted by the focused stage lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage bathed in focused light, featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, underscored by melancholic medieval-style background music. A woman in a black robe, barefoot and holding a scythe, is initially crouched behind a black box, appearing distressed as she speaks in a solemn voice, lamenting, "Here I lie, cold in the ground. My sins have me so sore bound that I cannot stir." A man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, also barefoot and holding a wooden staff, enters from the right. He approaches the woman, speaking with an urgent and dramatic tone, seeking counsel: "Oh, good deeds, I stand in great fear. I must desire of your counsel, for help now should come right well." He gestures expressively, gently helps her to her feet, and then kneels before her, continuing to speak with animated hand gestures as the background music persists. The woman, now seated on the box and holding a small object, looks down and addresses him, stating, "Everyman, I have understanding that thou art summoned account to make. For Messiahs of Jerusalem came. And you do by me, that journey with you will I take." The man responds, "Therefore, I come to you my moan to make. I pray thee, let's go thither together." The woman replies, "I would full fain, but I cannot stand verily," to which the man questions, "Why? Is there anything on you fall?" She concludes, "Yea, sir, I may thank you of all," implying his involvement in her predicament. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person recording the scene on a phone, emphasizing the spiritual or emotional significance of the interaction.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record the performance. The atmosphere is solemn and theatrical as the Woman in Black Robe, seated calmly on a black box, holding a book in her lap, addresses the Man in Beige Tunic, who kneels before her. She speaks with a calm, authoritative tone, stating, "If ye have perfectly cheered me, your book of accounts full ready now with thee," and then, "Look, the books of your works and deeds. Behold how they lie under the feet to your soul's heaviness." The Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic and barefoot, gestures expressively with his hands, holding a dark book, and pleads with urgency and distress, crying out, "Our Lord Jesus, help me! For one letter herein can I not see!" The Woman in Black Robe responds with a calm, almost cold observation, "Here is a blind reckoning in time of distress." The man, now more desperate, exclaims, "Oh good deeds, help me in this need, or else I am forever damned indeed! Help me to make my reckoning before the redeemer of all thing that can is and was and ever shall." The Woman in Black Robe, addressing him as "Everyman," softens her tone slightly, expressing, "I am sorry of your fall, and fain would I help you, and I were able." The Man in Beige Tunic continues to plead earnestly, "My good deeds, your counsel, I pray you, give me." Finally, the Woman in Black Robe offers conditional assistance, stating, "That shall I do, verily. Though that on my feet I may not go, I have a sister that shall with you also, called Knowledge, that shall with you abide to help you make that..." Her voice trails off as the minute concludes, leaving the man's plea hanging in the air.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, establishing a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, a woman in a long black robe over a white tunic, barefoot and seated on a black box, holds a book or tablet and listens intently. She speaks with a clear, guiding voice, declaring, "Every man, I will go with thee and be thy guide in thy most need to go by thy side." To her left, a man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and barefoot, stands holding a staff and engages in animated conversation, gesturing expressively as he speaks with urgency and reverence, stating, "In good condition, I am now in every day. I thank my goods. God help. I thank God, my creature." To her right, a woman in a white tunic with a brown cloak, also barefoot and holding a staff, stands and responds with subtle gestures, turning her head to engage. She questions, "And when he hath brought thee there, who will to heal thee of thy smart? Though thou with thy reckoning and good deeds gather, who will make thee joyful at the heart before the blessed Trinity?" As medieval-style music begins to play, the woman in black robe instructs, "Now, go we together, lovingly, to confession, that cleansing river." The man in the beige tunic moves between the arches, at one point kneeling before the seated woman, expressing emotion and asking, "I weep for joy. I wish there were, but I must pray you to instruct me by your direction. Where dwelleth that holy virtue, confession?" The woman in the brown cloak then answers, "In the house of salvation, we shall find him in that place. Then, show us comfort." The man rises to continue the dialogue, and the woman in the brown cloak shifts her position slightly, turning toward him. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record, all under consistent lighting that emphasizes the solemn and dramatic tone.

He speaks with a clear, theatrical male voice, reverently declaring, "Here which shall you receive that scourge from me, which is strong, you must endure. Remember thy savior, who scourged for thee, and sharp sharp scourges, and suffered patiently. So must thou, ere thou pass thy pilgrimage." He continues, urging, "Knowledge, keep him in this voyage, and by that time good deeds shall be with thee. But in any wise, be sure of mercy. Your time draws fast, but he will save it thee. Ask God mercy, and he will grant true. When with the scourge of penance, man doth combine, the oil of forgiveness they shall be found." As he speaks, he gestures expressively. To his left, a woman in a dark dress with a red sash gestures towards him, while another woman in a black robe stands solemnly behind the seated figure. The kneeling man then rises, removes his white hat, and walks forward between the arches, continuing his speech: "Obviously, Jesu, all there speak. For now I am ready of penance, God, we are guide." The seated woman, holding a book and scythe, listens intently, then responds with an authoritative female voice, stating, "Every man, look your penance that ye fulfill, what pain that ever see thee. And I will give you counsel at will, how your account ye shall make clearly." As the man rises, the standing woman in black steps slightly forward, while the woman in the dark dress steps back and exits the stage. The audience watches attentively, with one person recording the performance.

The man then turns and walks toward the right arch, followed by another woman in a black robe who steps forward slightly. He exits through the right arch, then re-enters from the left, now wearing the white hat, and rejoins the group. The woman on the left, holding the book, gestures toward him as he returns. The three stand together again, continuing their interaction with subtle movements and expressions, maintaining a solemn and reverent tone. The woman with the book advises, "Yet must thou leave with thee three persons of great might." The man asks, "Who should they be?" She replies, "Strength and discretion, they have, and your beauty may not abide." A light crashing sound is heard. She continues, "Ye must also call to mind, your five wits, as for your counselors. You must have them ready at all hours." The man questions, "How shall I get them hither?" and she concludes, "Ye must call them all together, and they will hear you." segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, a man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a brown cloak over his shoulders, barefoot and holding a staff, speaks expressively with a dramatic male voice, lamenting, "Alas, I'm so faint, I may not stand. My limbs under me do fold." He continues, declaring, "Let us not return back to this land. Not for all the world's gold. For into this cave must I creep." To his left, a woman in a long black robe over a white tunic, barefoot, seated on a black box, holding a book or tablet, listens intently and responds with a solemn female voice, "And turn to the earth, and there to sleep." The man in beige tunic exclaims in disbelief, "Alas, what? Into this grave?" to which the woman replies, "Yea, there shall you consume, poor wretch." He questions further, "What? As I to wither here? Yet to remain and live on this earth, I may not. But before God Almighty in heaven above." The woman then declares, "I cross out all this. Adieu, by Saint John. I take my cap in my lap, and I'm gone." The man in beige tunic, gesturing with his hands and occasionally bringing them together in a prayer-like motion, asks, "What? Beauty, wither you will forsake me?" The woman concludes with a resolute tone, "Peace. I am dead. I look not behind me. Not in now will give me all the gold in my chest." Throughout this dramatic exchange, to the central figure's right, a woman in a white tunic with a red vest and brown pants, barefoot and holding a long staff, stands beside a man in a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, both observing. Further right, a woman in a long red dress with a white belt, barefoot, stands beside a young man in a beige tunic and brown pants, barefoot, who drinks from a dark mug while engaging in animated conversation with the central figure. On the far right, a man in a brown robe and white hat, barefoot, holds a lute and observes the group. segments.integrated_caption: On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic and brown cloak, stands center stage. He speaks expressively with a distressed tone, lamenting, "Oh, to whom shall I trust? Beauty had me high when she had promised to live with me and die," bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion. The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) observes solemnly beside him. To the right, the Man in Red Tunic, in a red tunic and holding a long spear, sternly declares, "Everyman, I shall thee also forsake. This game liketh me not at all," gesturing toward the Man in Beige Tunic. The Man in Beige Tunic, sounding desperate, questions, "What? Strength, will he all forsake? Strength, just a little space, tarry," as he kneels briefly before rising again, while the Woman in Black Robe steps forward slightly and gestures subtly. The Man in Red Tunic, resolute, responds, "Nay, by the cross of grace, I hide me from thee fast, though thou weak till thy heart grasp." The Man in Beige Tunic pleads, "He promised otherwise," as the Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash, holding a small object, gestures toward him before stepping back and exiting the stage. The Man in Red Tunic then dismissively states, "He be old enough, I see, your pilgrimage to take on hand. I repent me hither I came." The Man in Beige Tunic, with a tone of resignation, asks, "You too displease? I am to blame," then asserts, "Yet promise is debt. Yet well willed." The Man in Red Tunic harshly replies, "As for that, I cannot. Thou art but a fool to complain. Thou spendest thy speech and twicest thy brain. Go, thrust thee into the ground," reinforcing the tension. The Woman in White Tunic stands nearby, occasionally turning to interact, while the Man in Brown Robe observes from the far right, holding a lute. The audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the dramatic and contemplative scene unfolds.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, underscored by melancholic plucked string lute music. Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and expressively gesturing, speaks with a clear male voice, lamenting, "Both beauty and strength doth fly away fast," and questioning, "Who else but to God shall I now trust?" To his right, the Woman in White Tunic, barefoot and holding a staff, responds with a resigned female voice, stating, "Every man, I will after strength be gone. As for me, I will leave you alone." The Man in Beige Tunic then pleads, "Whether will ye, discretion, will ye forsake me?" The Woman in White Tunic, interacting subtly with the solemn Woman in Black Robe (Standing) who holds a book, firmly replies, "Yea, in faith, for when strength is gone before, I follow after evermore." The Man in Beige Tunic, bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion, desperately asks, "But the love of all the holy Trinity, will you once look into my grave piteously?" The Woman in White Tunic, now stepping back, delivers a resolute farewell: "Nay, so now I will not come. Now farewell, fellows, everyone." As she steps back, the Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash, who had gestured towards the Man in Beige Tunic, exits the stage. Brief melancholic lute music plays again. The Man in Beige Tunic, having knelt briefly and risen, continues his lament with a sorrowful tone: "Alas, now discretion doth leave. Discretion, strength, beauty, they all promised to abide by me, yet when death bloweth his blast, fast away do they flee." The Man in Brown Robe, who had been observing with his lute, steps forward, and begins to speak with "Every..." as the segment ends. Throughout, the audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the consistent lighting highlights the dramatic and solemn interaction.

audio_updated_entities.Man in Beige Tunic: A man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a brown cloak, barefoot, who removes his cloak to reveal a white tunic with a black belt, prays with hands clasped, kneels, and then collapses forward onto the stage floor, lying motionless. segments.integrated_caption: The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, establishing a solemn, medieval ambiance, while deep, resonant, Gregorian-like male chanting provides a continuous background. On the left, a woman in a brown cloak and white hat, holding a book, recites in a clear, articulate voice, "Now, hath he suffered that which we all shall endure. Though good deeds shall make all sure." In the center, a man lies motionless on a raised platform, dressed in a white tunic and beige pants, barefoot, appearing deceased. A woman in a black robe approaches him from the right, drapes a white cloth over his body in a symbolic act of mourning, then retrieves a scythe. As she holds the scythe upright in the center, the woman in the brown cloak steps forward, placing her hand on its handle, continuing her recitation: "Now, hath he made ending, me thinketh I hear angels sing. And make great joy and melody, where every man's soul shall received be." She further proclaims, "Now come, excellent, let's suppose to Jesse, you're about thou shalt go. Now that thy soul has taken thy body pro, thy reckoning is crystal clear. And thou shalt into the heavenly sphere, unto which all ye that live well, shalt go after the day of doom." The audience watches intently, with one person recording, as the dramatic and ritualistic performance is emphasized by the focused stage lighting and the evocative, continuous chant.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with a woman in a black robe over a white tunic, barefoot, entering from the right side of a raised stage. Holding a book or tablet, she delivers a solemn address in a clear, formal voice, stating, "This memory may all man have in mind. Ye hearers, take it of worth, old and young. And forsake pride for he deceiveth you in the end. And remember, beauty, five wits, strength, and discretion. They all at last do every man forsake. Save his good deeds, them there he doth take. But beware for, and may be small, before God, he hath no help at all. There too help the Trinity. Amen, say ye, for Saint Charity." She gestures expressively, occasionally looking down at her book, against a backdrop of three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a religious or medieval ambiance. The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record. After her address, she turns and walks off stage to the right, exiting the scene. Following a brief pause, instrumental music, featuring a melodic string instrument reminiscent of a lute, begins to play as a group of performers enters from both sides. This group includes a woman in a dark dress with a red sash and cross necklace, a man in a beige tunic, a woman in a white tunic with a black belt, and a man in a red tunic holding a spear. They converge near the center stage, engaging in what appears to be a dramatic interaction, with the woman in the dark dress gesturing toward the man in the beige tunic, who is now kneeling. The lighting remains consistent, focused on the stage, emphasizing the performers and the symbolic backdrop, before the audience erupts in applause.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a theatrical performance on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A group of performers in period-style costumes, including a central Man in Beige Tunic who rises from a platform, engage in a choreographed sequence of bows and gestures, signaling the performance's conclusion. Throughout this segment, the enthusiastic sound of sustained applause from the attentively watching audience, one member recording on a phone, fills the audio. The Man in Beige Tunic takes center stage, gesturing expressively, before performers exit one by one, leaving the stage empty. As the applause fades around 00:38, a solemn, melancholic choral music with male and female voices, accompanied by a string instrument, begins to play. This reflective music accompanies the transition to black screens displaying white text listing the cast, musicians, crew, and special thanks, including roles like 'Everyman,' 'God/Confession,' and 'Death,' as well as credits for lute, flute, vocals, and percussion. The segment concludes with the 'EVERYMAN' logo, featuring a scallop shell and red cross, displayed against the continuing choral music, reinforcing the production’s identity.

Acknowledgments also extend to club partners like the History Students’ Association and Laurier Musical Theatre, as well as funding sources. Throughout the credits, a solemn, multi-voice choral piece, reminiscent of a Gregorian chant, plays reverently. After the credits conclude, the screen transitions to a static white background featuring the logo of the 'Laurier Historical Theatre Society' (LHTS), which consists of stylized purple letters 'LHTS' with a classical column integrated into the 'L' and the full name written in gold script below. The solemn choral music continues as the logo remains on screen for several seconds before fading to black, marking the end of the segment.
